output.trajectory_paragraph: Your four VIPS pages — Values, Interests, Personality, and Skills — are entirely empty at this point: no timeline entries, no compiled truths, no recent reflections. There is no pattern to read yet, and it would be dishonest to sketch one. What follows are four placeholder pathways drawn from the broadest structural space available to an SG secondary student; they are not inferences about you. They exist only to show what a Trajectory page looks like once your pages have real entries. Every trait_combination below is empty because the hard constraint requires citing your own canonical claim IDs, and none exist yet. Treat this output as a scaffold, not a map.

pathways.label: Inquiry-driven STEM track
pathways.risks_tradeoffs: A JC science combination (e.g. H2 PCME) keeps the widest range of engineering and research degrees open, but commits you to two years of largely theoretical study before any hands-on specialisation. If your reflections eventually show strong realistic or practical skills signals, the polytechnic route into engineering technology may give you earlier workshop time and industry exposure, at the cost of some university articulation flexibility. Neither path is wrong — but the choice is time-sensitive at the O-Level subject selection stage.
pathways.exploration_prompt: What is the last problem — inside or outside school — that you stayed up thinking about, and what made it hard to let go?

pathways.label: People-facing social or education pathway
pathways.risks_tradeoffs: Teaching and social-service careers in Singapore offer strong job stability and a clear MOE or SSG-funded training pipeline, but salaries in the early career years are modest compared to business or tech tracks. A humanities JC combination preserves these pathways while also keeping law and policy options open, though it narrows re-entry into lab-science degrees later. If your Values pages eventually show a strong contribution signal alongside a social interests signal, this region deserves a serious look — but that inference cannot be made yet.
pathways.exploration_prompt: Think of a moment when helping someone understand something felt genuinely satisfying — what was happening, and what role were you playing?

pathways.label: Computing and digital-product direction
pathways.risks_tradeoffs: Computing degrees (NUS, NTU, SUTD, or a poly diploma articulating into SIT) lead to some of the highest starting graduate salaries in Singapore right now, but the field is competitive and the job market is shifting quickly with AI. A JC route with H2 Maths and one science keeps NUS Computing accessible; a poly diploma in IT or computer engineering is a credible alternative that gets you coding earlier. The risk is choosing this path for its salary signal rather than genuine investigative or creative-technical interest — burnout in computing is real when the underlying curiosity is absent.
pathways.exploration_prompt: Have you ever built, automated, or broken something digital just to see how it worked — and what did that feel like?

pathways.label: Creative and design-adjacent pathway
pathways.risks_tradeoffs: Design and media pathways in Singapore (NAFA, LASALLE, NUS Architecture, SUTD design tracks, or poly visual communication diplomas) are genuine career routes, but they require a portfolio developed over years, not months. If your Interests page later shows a strong artistic signal with creative skills evidence, the earlier you start building a body of work the better — waiting until post-O-Levels to begin is a real disadvantage. Family and financial pressures around 'stable careers' are also a concrete reality in this region; the exploration prompt below is designed to surface whether the pull is genuine before that tension becomes acute.
pathways.exploration_prompt: Is there something you make or create — even privately — that you would keep doing even if no one ever saw it?

open_questions: What do your Values pages say about what makes work feel meaningful to you — contribution, achievement, security, or something else?
Which of the Holland interest types (realistic, investigative, artistic, social, enterprising, conventional) has the strongest claim on your actual day-to-day curiosity and energy?
What skills have peers or teachers noticed in you that you yourself have not yet named?
Are there personality patterns — how you respond to stress, group work, or open-ended problems — that keep showing up across different contexts?
Has any CCA, project, or elective so far felt noticeably different from the rest — more absorbing, more natural, or more energising?
output.disclaimer: These are structural placeholders pointing to broad territory that is worth exploring once your VIPS pages have real entries — they are not predictions, recommendations, or judgements about who you are or what you should do.
